|cult_centre={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en}}|cult_center={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en}}|member_of=[[Izumo clan]]|caption={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en}}, a cult center of {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft}}}} {{complex}} {{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} <!-- {{AFC comment|1=took from {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}}{{ill|User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le|en}} ❤️💚💙 {{ill|User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)|en}} 05:09, 5 May 2023 (UTC)}} -->
{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en||fr}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|user:immanuelle/reed bed}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson korin|en}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
[[ja:建比良鳥命]] == Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[reed bed]]s''}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

revision.text:
{{draft}}
{{Expand Japanese|建比良鳥命|建比良鳥命=建比良鳥命}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Takehi-Nateru | image =File:Washinomiya Shrine - new gate.jpg | god_of = [[Birds|Birds]] | father = {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}}<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a" /><ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> | consort = | siblings = {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}<ref name="Brown-1988" /> | relatives = [[Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (uncle) [[Amatsuhikone]] (uncle) [[Ikutsuhikone]] (uncle) [[Kumanokusubi]] (uncle)
|cult_centre={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en}}|cult_center={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en}}|member_of=[[Izumo clan]]|caption={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en}}, a cult center of {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft}}}} {{complex}} {{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} <!-- {{AFC comment|1=took from {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}}{{ill|User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le|en}} ❤️💚💙 {{ill|User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)|en}} 05:09, 5 May 2023 (UTC)}} -->
{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en||fr}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|user:immanuelle/reed bed}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson korin|en}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
[[ja:建比良鳥命]] == Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[reed bed]]s''}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== References == {{reflist}} [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[Category:Izumo clan]][[Category:Japanese gods]][[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]] [[Category:Simple english wikipedia and everybodywiki]] Category:original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]][[zh:建比良鳥命]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]] [[zh:建比良鳥命]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]]

|cult_centre={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en}}|cult_center={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en}}|member_of=[[Izumo clan]]|caption={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en}}, a cult center of {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft}}}} {{complex}} {{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} <!-- {{AFC comment|1=took from {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}}{{ill|User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le|en}} ❤️💚💙 {{ill|User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)|en}} 05:09, 5 May 2023 (UTC)}} -->
{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en||fr}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|user:immanuelle/reed bed}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson korin|en}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
[[ja:建比良鳥命]] == Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[reed bed]]s''}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== References == {{reflist}} [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[Category:Izumo clan]][[Category:Japanese gods]][[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]] [[Category:Simple english wikipedia and everybodywiki]] Category:original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]][[zh:建比良鳥命]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]] [[zh:建比良鳥命]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]]

{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en||fr}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|user:immanuelle/reed bed}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson korin|en}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
[[ja:建比良鳥命]] == Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[reed bed]]s''}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== References == {{reflist}} [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Takehi-Nateru-qq]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Takehi-Nateru-qq]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[Category:Izumo clan]][[Category:Japanese gods]][[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Takehi-Nateru-qq]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Takehi-Nateru-qq]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Takehi-Nateru-qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]] [[Category:Simple english wikipedia and everybodywiki]] [[Category:qq-]][[Category:qq]] Category:original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]][[zh:建比良鳥命]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]] [[zh:建比良鳥命]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]]

{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en||fr}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|user:immanuelle/reed bed}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson korin|en}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
[[ja:建比良鳥命]] == Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[reed bed]]s''}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
[[ja:建比良鳥命]] == Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[reed bed]]s''}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en||fr}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|user:immanuelle/reed bed}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson korin|en}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
[[ja:建比良鳥命]] == Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[reed bed]]s''}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

The entire simple english wikipedia history is present, but the wikipedia draft can be found on everybodywiki here [[every:Takehi-Nateru|Takehi-Nateru]] and should be undeleted here on wikipedia [[:en:draft:Takehi-Nateru]][[en:draft:Takehi-Nateru]] and have the revisions imported to this wiki to give proper attribution. While most edits are by Immanuelle there might be a few from other users. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 18 February 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|1=page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then copied to userspace on simple english wikipedia and edited before being imported here. The entire simple english wikipedia history is present, but the wikipedia draft can be found on everybodywiki here [[every:Takehi-Nateru|Takehi-Nateru]] and should be undeleted here on wikipedia [[:en:draft:Takehi-Nateru]][[en:draft:Takehi-Nateru]] and have the revisions imported to this wiki to give proper attribution. While most edits are by Immanuelle there might be a few from other users. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 18 February 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Expand Japanese|建比良鳥命|建比良鳥命=建比良鳥命}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Takehi-Nateru | image =File:Washinomiya Shrine - new gate.jpg | god_of = [[Birds|Birds]] | father = {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}}<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a" /><ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> | consort = | siblings = {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}<ref name="Brown-1988" /> | relatives = [[Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (uncle) [[Amatsuhikone]] (uncle) [[Ikutsuhikone]] (uncle) [[Kumanokusubi]] (uncle)
|cult_centre={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en}}|cult_center={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en}}|member_of=[[Izumo clan]]|caption={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en}}, a cult center of {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft}}}} {{complex}} {{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} <!-- {{AFC comment|1=took from {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}}{{ill|User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le|en}} ❤️💚💙 {{ill|User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)|en}} 05:09, 5 May 2023 (UTC)}} -->
{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en||fr}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|user:immanuelle/reed bed}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson korin|en}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
[[ja:建比良鳥命]] == Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[reed bed]]s''}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== References == {{reflist}} [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Takehi-Nateru-qq]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Takehi-Nateru-qq]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[Category:Izumo clan]][[Category:Japanese gods]][[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Takehi-Nateru-qq]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Takehi-Nateru-qq]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Takehi-Nateru-qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]][[Category:Takehi-Nateru]] [[Category:Simple english wikipedia and everybodywiki]] [[Category:qq-]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] Category:original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]][[zh:建比良鳥命]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]] [[zh:建比良鳥命]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru]]

{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en||fr}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|user:immanuelle/reed bed}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson korin|en}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
[[ja:建比良鳥命]] == Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[reed bed]]s''}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

|cult_centre={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en}}|cult_center={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en}}|member_of=[[Izumo clan]]|caption={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en}}, a cult center of {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft}}}} {{complex}}
<!-- {{AFC comment|1=took from {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} {{ill|User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le|en}} ❤️💚💙 {{ill|User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)|en}} 05:09, 5 May 2023 (UTC)}} -->
{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en||fr}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|user:immanuelle/reed bed}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson korin|en}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
[[ja:建比良鳥命]] == Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[reed bed]]s''}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en||fr}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|user:immanuelle/reed bed}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson korin|en}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
[[ja:建比良鳥命]] == Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[reed bed]]s''}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en||fr}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|user:immanuelle/reed bed}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson korin|en}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
[[ja:建比良鳥命]] == Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[reed bed]]s''}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en||fr}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|user:immanuelle/reed bed}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson korin|en}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
[[ja:建比良鳥命]] == Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[reed bed]]s''}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en||fr}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|user:immanuelle/reed bed}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson korin|en}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
[[ja:建比良鳥命]] == Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[reed bed]]s''}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en||fr}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|user:immanuelle/reed bed}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson korin|en}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
[[ja:建比良鳥命]] == Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[reed bed]]s''}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
