{{Expand Japanese|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|建比良鳥命=建比良鳥命}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Takehi-Nateru | image =File:Washinomiya Shrine - new gate.jpg | god_of = [[:en:Birds|Birds]] | father = {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}}<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a" /><ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> | consort = | siblings = {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}<ref name="Brown-1988" />|relatives =[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (uncle) [[Amatsuhikone]] (uncle) [[Ikutsuhikone]] (uncle) [[Kumanokusubi]] (uncle); {{ill|Ijimi no Wakugo|WD=Q24875715}}; {{ill|Unagami no Isachi|WD=Q11558664}}|cult_centre={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Washinomiya Shrine|qq=}}|cult_center={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Washinomiya Shrine|qq=}}|member_of=[[Izumo clan]]|caption={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Washinomiya Shrine|qq=}}, a cult center of {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}| parents = {{ill|Ame no Hohi|WD=Q10940685}}| offspring = {{ill|Isetsuhiko|WD=Q17193127}}; {{ill|Kushiyatama|WD=Q86734749|1=Kushiyatama-no-kami}}| equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Tori no Iwakusufune no Kami|WD=Q10731425}}}}
{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|User:Immanuelle/reed bed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/reed bed|qq=}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson korin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenson korin|qq=}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[:en:Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[:en:Reed bed|Reed bed]]s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== References == {{reflist|qq=draft}}

So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|User:Immanuelle/reed bed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/reed bed|qq=}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson korin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenson korin|qq=}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[:en:Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[:en:Reed bed|Reed bed]]s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[:en:Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[:en:Reed bed|Reed bed]]s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed korin|qq=}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[:en:Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />

== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[:en:Reed bed|Reed bed]]s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|User:Immanuelle/reed bed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/reed bed|qq=}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson korin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenson korin|qq=}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[:en:Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[:en:Reed bed|Reed bed]]s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|User:Immanuelle/reed bed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/reed bed|qq=}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson korin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenson korin|qq=}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[:en:Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[:en:Reed bed|Reed bed]]s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== References == {{reflist|qq=draft}}

== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[:en:Reed bed|Reed bed]]s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|User:Immanuelle/reed bed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/reed bed|qq=}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson korin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenson korin|qq=}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[:en:Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[:en:Reed bed|Reed bed]]s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

{{Expand Japanese|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|建比良鳥命=建比良鳥命}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Takehi-Nateru | image =File:Washinomiya Shrine - new gate.jpg | god_of = [[:en:Birds|Birds]] | father = {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}}<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a" /><ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> | consort = | siblings = {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}<ref name="Brown-1988" />|relatives =[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (uncle) [[Amatsuhikone]] (uncle) [[Ikutsuhikone]] (uncle) [[Kumanokusubi]] (uncle); {{ill|Ijimi no Wakugo|WD=Q24875715}}; {{ill|Unagami no Isachi|WD=Q11558664}}|cult_centre={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Washinomiya Shrine|qq=}}|cult_center={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Washinomiya Shrine|qq=}}|member_of=[[Izumo clan]]|caption={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Washinomiya Shrine|qq=}}, a cult center of {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}| parents = {{ill|Ame no Hohi|WD=Q10940685}}| offspring = {{ill|Isetsuhiko|WD=Q17193127}}; {{ill|Kushiyatama|WD=Q86734749|1=Kushiyatama-no-kami}}| equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Tori no Iwakusufune no Kami|WD=Q10731425}}}}
{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|User:Immanuelle/reed bed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/reed bed|qq=}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson korin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenson korin|qq=}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]], [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba]], and [[:en:Tsushima Province|Tsushima]].<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of [[:en:Reed bed|Reed bed]]s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo]] and [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba]] honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

korin|qq=}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}}, {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba}}, and {{ill|Tsushima Province|en|Tsushima Province|WD=Q1054861|lt=Tsushima}}.<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|Reed bed|en|Reed bed|WD=Q620424|lt=Reed bed}}s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}, Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

{{Expand Japanese|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|建比良鳥命=建比良鳥命}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Takehi-Nateru | image =File:Washinomiya Shrine - new gate.jpg | god_of = [[:en:Birds|Birds]] | father = {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}}<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a" /><ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> | consort = | siblings = {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}<ref name="Brown-1988" />|relatives =[[Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (uncle) [[Amatsuhikone]] (uncle) [[Ikutsuhikone]] (uncle) [[Kumanokusubi]] (uncle); {{ill|Ijimi no Wakugo|WD=Q24875715}}; {{ill|Unagami no Isachi|WD=Q11558664}}|cult_centre={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Washinomiya Shrine|qq=}}|cult_center={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Washinomiya Shrine|qq=}}|member_of=[[Izumo clan]]|caption={{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Washinomiya Shrine|qq=}}, a cult center of {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}}| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}| parents = {{ill|Ame no Hohi|WD=Q10940685}}| offspring = {{ill|Isetsuhiko|WD=Q17193127}}; {{ill|Kushiyatama|WD=Q86734749|1=Kushiyatama-no-kami}}| equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Tori no Iwakusufune no Kami|WD=Q10731425}}}}
{{ill|Takehi-Nateru|ja|建比良鳥命|1=Takehi-Nateru|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|en:draft|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}} (建比良鳥命) is a Japanese god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|User:Immanuelle/reed bed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/reed bed|qq=}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson korin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenson korin|qq=}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}}, {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba}}, and {{ill|Tsushima Province|en|Tsushima Province|WD=Q1054861|lt=Tsushima}}.<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|Reed bed|en|Reed bed|WD=Q620424|lt=Reed bed}}s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}, Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165525/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9146|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="web.archive.org-2023a">{{Cite web|date=2023-06-05|title=Amenohohi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605104153/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9434|access-date=2024-03-24|website=web.archive.org}}</ref><ref name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|User:Immanuelle/reed bed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/reed bed|qq=}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson korin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenson korin|qq=}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}}, {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba}}, and {{ill|Tsushima Province|en|Tsushima Province|WD=Q1054861|lt=Tsushima}}.<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|Reed bed|en|Reed bed|WD=Q620424|lt=Reed bed}}s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}, Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== References == {{reflist|qq=draft}}

Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|Reed bed|en|Reed bed|WD=Q620424|lt=Reed bed}}s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

Today, shrines in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

name="shintoshrines">{{Cite web|title=The Gods of Japan {{!}} Amaterasu-Ōkami 天照大御神 {{!}}Susanoo-no-Mikoto 須佐之男命|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/amasusa|access-date=2024-03-24|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref><ref name="More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan">{{Cite web|title=More glimpses of unfamiliar Japan|url=https://www.ojisanjake.com/2014/03/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=en}}</ref><ref name="英訳 出雲神話">{{Cite web|title=英訳 出雲神話/English Translation Izumo Myths::２．天の安の河の誓約-出雲神話翻訳研究会/The Man From Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|User:Immanuelle/reed bed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/reed bed|qq=}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson korin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenson korin|qq=}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}}, {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba}}, and {{ill|Tsushima Province|en|Tsushima Province|WD=Q1054861|lt=Tsushima}}.<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|Reed bed|en|Reed bed|WD=Q620424|lt=Reed bed}}s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}, Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

Izumo tells the Izumo-myths of the Kojiki|url=http://izumo-kojiki.com/izumomyths/amaterasu/100/|access-date=2024-03-24|language=ja}}</ref> one of the five male kami (divine beings) born from a special event known as the "trial by pledge" between two major deities, Amaterasu and Susanoo. {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was sent to the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|reed bed|en|lt=reed beds|reed bed|simple|User:Immanuelle/reed bed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/reed bed|qq=}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson korin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenson korin|qq=}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}}, {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba}}, and {{ill|Tsushima Province|en|Tsushima Province|WD=Q1054861|lt=Tsushima}}.<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|Reed bed|en|Reed bed|WD=Q620424|lt=Reed bed}}s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}, Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

bed|simple|User:Immanuelle/reed bed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/reed bed|qq=}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson korin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenson korin|qq=}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}}, {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi|1=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, and {{ill|Tsushima Province|en|Tsushima Province|WD=Q1054861|lt=Tsushima|1=Tsushima Province}}.<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|Reed bed|en|Reed bed|WD=Q620424|lt=Reed bed|1=Reed bed}}s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}, Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba|1=Inaba Province}} honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}}, {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi|1=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, and {{ill|Tsushima Province|en|Tsushima Province|WD=Q1054861|lt=Tsushima|1=Tsushima Province}}.<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|Reed bed|en|Reed bed|WD=Q620424|lt=Reed bed|1=Reed bed}}s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}, Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba|1=Inaba Province}} honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
