bed|simple|User:Immanuelle/reed bed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/reed bed|qq=}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson korin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenson korin|qq=}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}}, {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi|1=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, and {{ill|Tsushima Province|en|Tsushima Province|WD=Q1054861|lt=Tsushima|1=Tsushima Province}}.<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|Reed bed|en|Reed bed|WD=Q620424|lt=Reed bed|1=Reed bed}}s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}, Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba|1=Inaba Province}} honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />

god and son of {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}},<ref name="web.archive.org-2023">{{Cite web|date=2023-03-21|title=Takehiratori bed|simple|User:Immanuelle/reed bed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/reed bed|qq=}}''}} to prepare for the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson korin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenson korin|qq=}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
He is the ancestor of many prominent families in, {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}}, {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi|1=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, and {{ill|Tsushima Province|en|Tsushima Province|WD=Q1054861|lt=Tsushima|1=Tsushima Province}}.<ref name="shintoshrines" /><ref name="英訳 出雲神話" /> He started the Fire fesival of [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="Brown-1988" />
Ame no Hohi's other son is {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakahiko|qq=}}.<ref name="Brown-1988">{{Cite book |last1=Brown |first1=Delmer M. |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Cambridge_History_of_Japan/A3_6lp8IOK8C?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Ame+no+Hohi&pg=PA346&printsec=frontcover |title=The Cambridge History of Japan |last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney |last3=Brown |first3=Delmer Myers |last4=Press |first4=Cambridge University |last5=Jansen |first5=Marius B. |last6=McCullough |first6=William H. |last7=Shively |first7=Donald H. |last8=Yamamura |first8=Kozo |last9=Duus |first9=Peter |date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |pages=346 |language=en}}</ref> He is also known by the name {{ill|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|en|Takehi-Nateru|lt=Takehi-Nateru|lt=Ame-no-Hinadori|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hinadori|qq=}}, contrary to popular belief that is not the name of an additional son.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Names == Takehi-Nateru, also known by several other names such as Takehiratori no Mikoto, Takehinatori no Mikoto, and Ame no Hinatori.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Role in Mythology == In the Kojiki, the oldest Japanese book, Takehi-Nateru is mentioned as an ancestor to the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. Both the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], narrate how {{ill|Ame no Hohi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}} was tasked with preparing the {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}'''|葦原の中つ国|''The middle country of {{ill|Reed bed|en|Reed bed|WD=Q620424|lt=Reed bed|1=Reed bed}}s''|qq=draft}} but ultimately did not succeed in his mission.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
So Takehi-Nateru and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} went down to pacify {{ill|Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|qq=}}. They managed to subdue unruly deities and persuaded a prominent deity, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]], to leave the earthly realm. After their victory the heavenly grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended to the Earth and Takehi-Nateru became the ancestor of the [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], who ruled {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
== Legacy == According to the Nihongi's account of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}, Takehi-Nateru descended from heaven with divine treasures.<ref name="Brown-1988" /><ref name="web.archive.org-2023" /> These treasures were kept at [[Izumo-taisha]] and later handed over to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}. However, this transfer led to conflicts among the Izumo, Furune, and Iiirine family branches, who were the keepers of these sacred items. Today, shrines in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba|1=Inaba Province}} honor Takehi-Nateru.<ref name="web.archive.org-2023" />
